strengths: Clear identification of Mr Xâs workflow weaknesses and solid evidence of client consultation via the interview transcript
Thorough, client-centred justification for choosing Java/PostgreSQL with hashing and AES over Google Sheets
Eleven specific, measurable success criteria covering all core functions
weaknesses: Limited evidence of iterative follow-up questions or clarifications after the initial interview
Minor gaps in tying every consultation point back to development decisions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The planning is strong across all strands: scenario and consultation are clearly described (band 5), the product rationale is fully justified (band 5), and success criteria are comprehensive (band 6). A small deduction (â1) reflects the absence of documented iterative follow-up discussions, placing planning at 5/6.

strengths: Effective integration of Java Swing, JDBC, AES and SHA-256 libraries with secure practices (e.g., prepared statements)
Well-explained encryption/decryption methods and error-handling approach
Clear documentation of code origin with a bibliography and annotated screenshots
weaknesses: Complexity is moderate rather than highly original (standard libraries used)
Several code snippets contain typos or minor syntactic errors
Bibliography entries lack uniform formatting and some techniques are under-referenced
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The development shows appropriate and effective use of tools (band 9/12), moderate complexity and ingenuity (band 7/12), and adequate documentation (band 6/12). An overall deduction of 3 points from the maximum reflects minor errors and the use of standard rather than novel algorithms, yielding 6/12.

strengths: Comprehensive wire-frame mock-ups for every screen, flow-charts mapping major processes, a full data dictionary and ERD
Ten detailed test cases linked directly to success criteria
weaknesses: No record of tasks or development log/Gantt chart provided
Several test templates remain blank or are only partially populated
Flow-chart branches lack decision labels and some mock-ups lack captions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Design overview and test plan are both in the top band (5/6), but the complete absence of any record of tasks is a major omission (band 0). Balancing two strong strands against one critical gap yields a mid-high mark of 3/6.

strengths: Evidence (screenshots and narrative) indicates all core features function as intended
Database design is normalized with clear structures supporting straightforward expansion
weaknesses: No video demonstration of live functionality
Functionality proof relies solely on static documentation and narrative
Minor missing features (e.g., no explicit failure-mode screenshots)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Documentation and screenshots support that the product functions well (band 3â4) and is straightforward to extend (band 3â4). The lack of a live demo or video leads to a one-point deduction, awarding 3/4.

strengths: Evaluation revisits all eleven success criteria with verdicts and supporting comments
Includes genuine client feedback comments from Mr X
weaknesses: Recommendations section is extremely limited (only one item) and lacks breadth
One client comment is truncated, reducing clarity
No adviser perspective or iterative feedback amending the product
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the success-criteria evaluation is comprehensive (band 5â6) and there is some client feedback (band 3â4), the future-recommendations strand is weak (band 1â2). The overall evaluation is pulled down by trivial recommendations and incomplete feedback, earning 2/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: The design section supplies wire-frame mock-ups for every screen, several flow-charts covering major processes, a data dictionary and an ER-diagram.  Collectively they convey a clear and complete picture of structure and navigation, satisfying the descriptors for a comprehensive overview.

The student fully explains why a dedicated Java/PostgreSQL password manager, with hashing and AES, is preferable to a shared spreadsheet.  Advantages are linked to the clientâs security and usability demands and alternative approaches (staying with Sheets) are dismissed with clear reasoning.  The justification is thorough and client-centred, meriting the top band.

Java Swing, PostgreSQL, prepared statements, AES and SHA-256 libraries are used effectively and safely (e.g., prepared statements to mitigate SQL injection).  The student shows competence in integrating these tools, which aligns with âappropriate and effective useâ; a low mark within the top band is therefore appropriate.

Ten separate tests are tabulated, each linking data, procedure and expected outcome to the success criteria.  Although formatting is occasionally untidy, coverage is broad and would enable systematic verification, meeting the âdetailed and completeâ description.

One practical suggestion (âcopy to clipboardâ) is recorded and rationalised in terms of security and usability, making it realistic.  The recommendations section is short and not wide-ranging, so mid-band (3) is most fitting.

A bibliography cites online articles and video tutorials, and screenshots annotate the origin of code segments.  Techniques such as hashing and encryption are explained, but not every external snippet is fully referenced.  This merits a secure mid-band mark rather than the uppermost scores.
No iterative âRecord of Tasksâ or Gantt-style log is provided.  Consequently there is no visible evidence of how the product evolved over time, placing the work in band 0.

The product integrates GUI construction, JDBC database access, SHA-256 hashing, AES encryption/decryption, and CRUD SQL operations, demonstrating more than basic programming.  However, algorithmic originality remains moderate (standard libraries used), so a solid mid-band (â7) is fitting rather than the very top.

A list of eleven specific, measurable criteria covers login, CRUD operations, ordering, search, encryption/decryption and data protection.  Together they form a comprehensive yard-stick for success, so the highest mark in the band is appropriate.

The transcript and summary capture genuine client remarks both before and after testing, but feedback is brief and lacks iteration or adviser perspective.  This fulfils âsome feedbackâ, placing it in the 3-4 band at its lower end.

Data are stored in a normalised database; the use of sequences and clear table structure means adding new fields or modules would be straightforward.  The solution is therefore extensible, matching the highest band for this strand.

The problem description clearly identifies Mr X, outlines his present workflow, and specifies concrete weaknesses of the current Google-Sheet approach.  An appendix contains a multi-question interview transcript that demonstrates genuine two-way consultation.  Detail is adequate to guide development, but a little more evidence of iterative follow-up questions would be needed for the very top of the band.

Screenshots and the evaluation narrative imply that all core features operate correctly (login gate, CRUD, search, encryption/decryption).  In absence of a video the assessor relies on this documented evidence, which supports the âfunctions wellâ descriptor.

Each of the eleven criteria is revisited with a succinct judgement and supporting comment, demonstrating a complete evaluation.  Although depth per criterion is brief, coverage is exhaustive, justifying a high-band placement (5).

annotations.comment: The problem definition clearly identifies Mr Xâs role and his current insecure use of Google Sheets, providing a solid foundation for the proposed solution.
The rationale articulates strong client-centred justification for using Java, PostgreSQL, SHA-256 and AES over a spreadsheet, linking features to Mr Xâs security and usability needs.
To strengthen the evidence of client consultation, include brief notes on iterative follow-up questions or clarifications elicited after the initial interview.
The eleven success criteria are highly specific and measurable, covering all core functionsâfrom login security to CRUD operations and encryptionâforming a comprehensive evaluation framework.
A record of tasks or Ganttâstyle log is missing; providing a timeline of milestones would demonstrate the iterative process and satisfy the recordâkeeping requirement.
The GUI prototype uses Moqups effectively to convey layout and navigation flow, giving a clear picture of the planned interface.
Annotate field labels and button behaviors in the prototype (e.g., disabled states, error messages) to guide development and testing.
Define password complexity requirements (length, character classes) in the setup screen prototype to ensure the clientâs security criteria are enforced.
Consider adding an errorâhandling path and user notification for database connectivity failures on the login screen to improve robustness and user feedback.
The separation of password creation and login screens clearly supports a logical user flow and enhances security by isolating phases.
The home tab prototype demonstrates intuitive search and list presentation, aligning well with usability objectives.
Specify whether search is caseâsensitive, supports partial matches, or uses regex to clarify expected behavior for testers.
The create/edit tab prototypes cover all CRUD operations with clear input fields and action buttons, aiding systematic development.
Add explicit validation design (e.g., invalid IDs, missing fields) to the form prototypes, including placeholder error dialogs.
The flowcharts effectively map user interactions and system processes, providing clear logic paths for development.
Include decision labels (e.g., yes/no, success/failure) on the flowchart branches to improve readability and traceability of each outcome.
The ERD visually conveys entity relationships well, but a brief narrative explaining cardinalities and key constraints would solidify the design overview.
All visual mock-ups should include concise captions or labels to orient readers and link interface elements to requirements.
Ensure the exported interface images maintain consistent resolution and alignment within the document to improve the professional appearance of the design overview.
The data dictionary lists all key fields with types, descriptions, and examples, offering a comprehensive reference for database implementation.
To enhance precision, define data formats (e.g., date patterns, numeric ranges) and constraints (NULL/NOT NULL, unique) for each field in the dictionary.
The ERD is wellâplaced with a clear caption; coupling it with the adjacent test plan introduction illustrates logical flow from design to verification.
The preliminary test table (Table 4) is empty; remove this placeholder or populate it to maintain consistency and avoid confusion.
The first two test cases are detailed with data sets, expected outcomes, and pass/fail evidence, enabling systematic verification.
Several later tests remain unpopulated; fill all test templates (#3â#10) or remove blanks to complete the test plan.
In the SQL snippet, the query uses `SELECT login password` instead of the correct `SELECT login_password`, which will cause a syntax error at runtime.
Apply tryâwith-resources or explicitly close connections, statements, and result sets in the code to prevent resource leaks and ensure robust error handling.
The DELETE statement snippet is clearly presented alongside descriptive text explaining its role, illustrating good alignment between code and functionality.
The 1D byte array for the AES key is well explained, highlighting retrieval from the database and preventing data corruption.
The tryâcatch block for password hashing and SQL insertion is described clearly, demonstrating attention to error management.
The encryption/decryption methods are thoroughly explained with Base64 encoding, supporting secure storage workflows.
There is a typographical error in the code snippet (`Baseb4.g<`); ensure the code block matches actual implementation for clarity.
The SHA-256 hashing explanation is concise and clearly justifies the use of hashing for storing the master password securely.
Organize import statements by grouping unused ones and alphabetizing to improve code readability and maintainability.
Bibliography entries lack consistent formattingâinclude author, date, title, source, and access dates in a uniform style.
The evaluation revisits all eleven success criteria with client feedback, demonstrating a complete assessment of the productâs performance.
One evaluation comment is truncated (âwhich Mr. X noted asâ); complete the clientâs observation for clarity and full context.
The recommendations section lists only one item; consider adding realistic enhancements such as role-based access control or mobile client support.
